3

Upper body

Overhead presses
Handstand push-ups
• For progressions, see this video.
• The exercise can be made more difficulty (for the true badasses) by wearing a
weighted vest.

Unilateral sumo dumbbell overhead press
• Use a pronated grip (externally rotated shoulder/dumbbell in line with your
shoulders) and press a single dumbbell overhead from a sumo stance.
• During the descent, keep your elbow out to the side as much as possible. Only let it
come forward as needed in the bottom to let the elbow come down as far as it can
for a maximal stretch.
• You can use your free arm as a counterbalance.
• Keep your knees just short of lock-out and keep an arched back.
• Your upper back should be arched as much as possible but your lower back should
stay neutral. It can help to contract your glutes in the bottom position to prevent
lumbar hyperextension (excessive arching of the lower back).
• Make sure your elbows stay directly below your wrists, and don't move the
dumbbells towards your shoulders. Let them come all the way down and slightly
forward until they touch your delts or until your elbows can't flex any further. You'll
probably need to lean back slightly in the bottom position to keep your elbows
directly under your wrists.
• Avoid using any leg drive.
• Perform a hang power clean to get the dumbbell into position for the first rep.

4

Barbell overhead press
• Do not lock out your knees: maintain a slight knee bend.
• Space your hands so wide that your forearms are perpendicular to the floor (vertical)
when the bar hits your collar bone/upper chest.
• Full range of motion requires that the bar touches your clavicle or upper chest in the
bottom; at the top, the bar should be pressed out directly above the middle of your
feet with straight arms.
• During the descent, keep your elbows out to the sides as much as possible. Only let
them come forward as needed in the bottom to let the elbows come down as far as
they can for a maximal stretch.
• Do not use any leg drive or jumping movement and do not duck under the barbell to
catch it. This is an upper body exercise.
• The bar path should be as vertical as possible. To achieve this, you'll have to move
your head around the barbell rather than move the barbell around your head.
• Your wrists should stay flexed and vertical, not slacked back, for maximal force
transfer.
• In the bottom position, maximally arch your upper back (thoracic spine) and push
your hips slightly forward, contracting your glutes if needed to avoid hyperextending
the lumbar spine.

5

The correct bottom and top position of the overhead press.

Standing dumbbell overhead press
• Keep your elbows directly below your wrists. To achieve this, your upper back
should generally be arched as much as possible in the bottom position. Your lower
back should remain neutral. To extend your thoracic but not your lumbar spine, flex
your glutes and push your hips forward in the bottom position.
• Don't move the dumbbells towards your shoulders. Let them come all the way down
with your elbows out to the sides and then slightly forward in the bottom position to
get your elbows down as far as possible. If needed, rotate the dumbbells on the way
down (supinating the wrist) to avoid having the dumbbells touch your shoulders and
limit your range of motion.
• To get the weights up for the first rep, you'll have to use some swing. Swing the
dumbbells back and then forward explosively. Quickly lunge forward and thrust your
elbows under the dumbbells to catch the weight in the starting position. Reposition
the dumbbells and press up the weight.

Arnold Press

6

• Perform standing and with one arm at a time (unilaterally), unless you strongly prefer
the bilateral execution or can't resist turning it into a bent press when using only one
arm.
• Start with the dumbbell at shoulder level and a supinated grip (hand's palm towards
your face). Then press up the weight while you rotate your hand outwards, ending in
the same position as a regular dumbbell overhead press.
• To hold your balance, use a sumo (wide) stance and stick out your free arm as a
counterbalance.
• Perform a hang power clean to get the dumbbell into position for the first rep.

Cable overhead press
• Stand exactly in between 2 cable pulleys and then take a small step forward so the
cable won't have to bend over your arms during the exercise.
• Let your elbows come down as far as possible in the bottom position. At the top, lock
out your elbows.
• Keep your forearms in line with the cable pushing angle (almost vertical).
• If you feel the movement is too heavy at the top and too easy in the bottom, increase
the height or width of the cable pulleys. Similarly, if you feel the movement is too
heavy in the bottom, almost requiring a biceps curl to keep the weights in position,
decrease the width or height of the cable pulleys.

7

Lateral raises
Side lying lateral raise
• Lean sideways on a high incline bench (approximately 45° incline), keep the
dumbbell just above your hip and raise it explosively straight out to your side,
maintaining tension on the deltoid at all times.
• If it feels comfortable, you can lift the arm all the way up until it's vertical, but there's
little muscle tension in that position, so it's fine to only go up about two thirds or even
just up to shoulder height.

Modified upright row
• To protect your shoulders from impingement, keep your chest up, bend over
forwards slightly and pull your elbows up and slightly back without letting them reach
above shoulder height.
• For wrist comfort, use an EZ-bar or a cable rope attachment.
• You can also perform these by standing with a rope attached to a low cable pulley at
a 45° angle with your body, standing upright in front of it.

Egyptian lateral raises
• Keep your arm (nearly) straight: do not allow any bending of the elbow to occur
during the exercise.
• Keep your chest up and bend over slightly forwards to protect your shoulders from
impingement and maximize range of motion.
• The cable should end up horizontally in line with your shoulders, not in front of them.
• If you experience a pronounced sticking point at the top, step back further away from
the cable tower to get relatively more resistance in the bottom and less at the top.
• Tempo: Explosively lift the weight, do not pause at the top, lower the weight under
control and pause for a split second in the bottom position before starting the next
rep.

8

Landmine lateral raise
You don't need a landmine apparatus for this. A barbell propped into a corner or a towel
can work fine.

Prone incline lateral raise (ignore video's instruction)
• Position the bench at a 60° incline; 45° if needed for full range of motion without
being obstructed at the bottom by the bench.
• Keep your arms completely straight and all the way out to the sides.
• Keep your thumbs pointed down at the floor.
• Explosively raise your elbows to shoulder height and then control the descent down
until they hang down fully vertical again.

Cable lateral raise
• Keep your arm (nearly) straight: do not allow any bending of the elbow to occur
during the exercise.
• Keep your chest up and bend over slightly forwards to protect your shoulders from
impingement and maximize range of motion.
• The cable should end up horizontally in line with your shoulders, not in front of them.
• If you experience a pronounced sticking point at the top, step back further away from
the cable tower to get relatively more resistance in the bottom and less at the top.

pages.text:
9

• Tempo: Explosively lift the weight, do not pause at the top, lower the weight under
control and pause for a split second in the bottom position before starting the next
rep.

Full cup lateral raise

• Move your arm up in a V-motion (the scapular plane). Your arms should come about
30° more forward than during a lateral raise where your arms go straight out to the
sides, so it's a slight front-lateral raise hybrid.
• Keep your thumbs pointed up at the ceiling.
• Don't let your shoulders relax in the bottom. Once your arms have come down under
control, immediately revert the movement and explode back up.
• 1980s IT guy outfit optional.

Butterfly lateral raise
• This is a dumbbell lateral raise with shoulder rotation to maximize range of motion.
• Rotate your shoulders externally (i.e. rotate your thumbs up) every rep. In the bottom
position your shoulders should be internally rotated (palms facing your body) and
you should be bent over forwards a little for full ROM. In the top position you should
be standing up straight with externally rotated shoulders (same end position as a
regular dumbbell overhead press). Your thumbs should be pointing towards the

10

ceiling when they're at around shoulder height, halfway through each rep. The same
ends of the dumbbells/plates should be able to touch other in the bottom and the top
position.
• Keep your arms straight.
• Keep your chest up to minimize shoulder impingement risk.

Lean-in lateral raises
• Lift the dumbbell up to shoulder height.
• Don't let the dumbbell hit your leg in the bottom position. Maintain muscle tension.
• Keep your arm straight out to the side.

11

Chest presses
45° Incline bench press (wide grip)
• Your grip should be as wide as possible while still having your forearms be
perpendicular perpendicular to the floor (vertical) when the bar hits your chest. Don't
turn it into a guillotine press though: if you can't get into the bottom position without
weight on the bar, it's too high up on your chest.
• Your upper back should be maximally arched and your shoulder blades should be
retracted and driven into the bench throughout the whole movement to protect your
shoulders from impingement.
• For how to use leg drive, watch this video.
• Squeeze the bar hard ('deathgrip') and keep your wrists directly above your elbows.

Cable chest press
• With a 45-60° angle between your arms and torso (angle of shoulder abduction),
press forward the cables from a maximal pec stretch to complete elbow lock-out.
• Press your chest forward during the exercise to protect your shoulders.
• A staggered stance and kneeling on the floor can help you stabilize the exercise with
heavier weights. With light weights you can still perform the exercise standing. With
heavy weights you are most stable lying down on a bench between 2 cable pulleys.
• Be sure to implement the same cable heights, trunk angle and position relative to the
cables every training session.

15° Incline dumbbell bench press
• Keep a 45-60° angle between your arms and upper body. The dumbbells should not
touch your chest in the bottom position. Flare your elbows out to prevent this. If the
dumbbells are particularly large, you may also need to rotate your wrists to prevent
the dumbbells from touching your chest and thereby limiting your range of motion.
• Your elbows should lock out at the top and go down as far as possible in the bottom.

12

• Your set-up should be just as tight as for a barbell bench press. Retract your
shoulder blades firmly into the bench and arch your upper back as much as
possible, pushing your chest up with your legs (leg drive). Think of pushing yourself
into the bench rather than pressing the dumbbells up.
• To get into the starting position for the first rep, start sitting upright with the
dumbbells on your thighs. Then you have to kick up the dumbbells with your knees
while you let yourself fall back on the bench. Some people can do this with straight
arms so they end with their arms straight overhead, but with heavy weights, if you
don't have shoulder problems, it may be safer to let the dumbbells fall into the
bottom position and then press them up immediately.

Flat dumbbell bench press [2]
• Keep a 45-60° angle between your arms and upper body. The dumbbells should not
touch your chest in the bottom position. Flare your elbows out to prevent this. If the
dumbbells are particularly large, you may also need to rotate your wrists to prevent
the dumbbells from touching your chest and thereby limiting your range of motion.
• Your elbows should lock out at the top and go down as far as possible in the bottom.
• Your set-up should be just as tight as for a barbell bench press. Retract your
shoulder blades firmly into the bench and arch your upper back as much as
possible, pushing your chest up with your legs (leg drive). Think of pushing yourself
into the bench rather than pressing the dumbbells up.
• To get into the starting position for the first rep, start sitting upright with the
dumbbells on your thighs. Then you have to kick up the dumbbells with your knees
while you let yourself fall back on the bench. Some people can do this with straight
arms so they end with their arms straight overhead, but with heavy weights, if you
don't have shoulder problems, it may be safer to let the dumbbells fall into the
bottom position and then press them up immediately.

Barbell bench press [2, 3]

13

• Your grip should be as wide as possible while still having your forearms vertical,
perpendicular to the floor, when the bar hits your lower chest or upper abs. If you
have sensitive shoulders, touch the bar nearer to your upper abdomen. If you have
no shoulder issues, touch the bar at or slightly your nipple line.
• Each rep should go from the bar touching your body to full elbow lock-out with your
arms vertical and the bar directly above your shoulders.
• Your upper back should be maximally arched and your shoulder blades should be
retracted and driven into the bench throughout the whole movement to protect your
shoulders and improve stability.
• Set your legs in a position from which you can push your chest up well without your
butt coming off the bench. Pushing on your legs should push your chest up and
provide a stable body position to press from. If you don't know how to use leg drive
yet, watch this video. If you don't care about how much you lift per se, don't use an
exaggerated powerlifting arch though, as it can reduce your range of motion.
• Squeeze the bar hard ('deathgrip') and keep your wrists directly above your elbows
at all times.
• As you lower the bar, tuck your elbows in a bit.
• During the press off your chest, push the bar up and back toward your face to get
the bar over your shoulders, allowing your elbows to flare out naturally ('J-curve'
press). You want a 45-60° angle between your upper arms and torso.

Push-ups
• If you're strong enough and you have push-up handles, use them to increase the
ROM and get a better stretch. You can also use dumbbells to create a deficit (hex
DBs work better than DBs that can roll) or, if you're up for it, perform the push-ups
on your knuckles.
• Place your hands below your shoulder line, as wide as possible while still having
vertical forearms in the bottom position.
• Keep your glutes tensed to prevent your stomach from draping down.
• Each rep should go from your chest touching the floor to full elbow lock-out.

14

• Load the exercise with a plate on your back (in a backpack, if you prefer). If you
have chains or bands, you can use those too, but don't use so much
accommodating resistance that it shifts up the sticking point to the top half of the
movement. Chains: diagonally drape them across your shoulders. Band: loop it
across your upper back just under your armpits.
When you can no longer practically increase the exercise loading, you can increase
the difficulty of the exercise in the following ways.
• Internally rotate your shoulders by using a Diamond hand position (hands
turned in up to 45°: the girl in the video has extreme flexibility).

• Put your feet on something with a height that is at most equal to the length
of your forearms to perform feet elevated push-ups.
• Progress to suspended push-ups (weighted with a dip belt). You can also
elevate your feet again. Note that it's about the elevation of your feet in
relation to your hands, not the absolute height. Go down as deep as
possible in the bottom and push yourself up to full elbow lock-out at the
top.

Smith-machine push-ups

15

• You can increase the exercise difficulty by adjusting the Smith machine barbell one
tooth down. After you reach the lowest position, switch to regular push-ups on the
floor.

16

Chest flys
Ring fly
• Keep your arms as straight as you can.
• Maintain a 45-60° angle between your torso and arms if a full 90° angle irritates your
shoulders.
• The exercise difficulty can be increased by elevating your feet (or lowering the
rings), wearing a weighted vest or putting plates in a backpack.

Dumbbell fly-press with eccentric overloading

Dumbbell flys
• Lower the dumbbells all the way down to a full pec stretch.
• Keep your arms slightly bent - almost fully straight - and fixed in that position. If it
feels comfortable and you have difficulty refraining from cheating, you can also keep
your arms straight. All movement should come from your shoulders without any
change in the degree of elbow flexion.
• Keep a 45-60° angle between your arms and torso to keep your shoulders safe.
Don't lower the weights straight out to the sides with a 90° shoulder abduction angle.

Bayesian fly
• This exercise can be performed standing, kneeling (more stable with heavy weights),
seated upright against a bench or lying on a bench. If you don't have 2 appropriately
aligned cable towers, you can perform this exercise unilaterally, i.e. with one arm at
a time.
• Keep your arms straight.
• To protect your shoulders from excessive impingement, keep the angle between
your arms and torso to 45-60°, not a full 90° guillotine style. The cable should be at
about nipple height in the stretched position.
• If you're interested in the detailed rationale for this exercise, see this article.

17

Butterfly pec deck flys
• Push from your elbows, not your hands.
• Keep your elbows slightly below shoulder height to protect your shoulders.
• Full range of motion entails your elbows go behind your body for a maximum pec
stretch and then you push them to each other until the machine handles touch each
other.

18

Triceps isolation exercises
Suspended triceps extensions [home/Smith variant]
• Practically all movement should come from the elbow, facilitated by some shoulder
movement to allow for a natural motion. It is a triceps extension, not a press.

Triceps pushdowns, forward leaning
•
Minimize elbow movement: focus on contracting your triceps instead of a pushing
movement.

Triceps pushdown, reverse grip, supinated

Triceps cross-over push-downs
• There's not always need for the scapular movement shown in the video or the
shoulder extension: you can simply perform the push-down movement without any
movement at the shoulder if that feels fine.
• Experiment with the distance from the cable pulleys, the angle of pull and leaning
forward to see what's most comfortable for your elbows.

Skull-overs
• Avoid making too much of a pull-over motion: this is primarily a triceps exercise.
• Your elbow angle should fully close so that in the bottom position your biceps touch
your forearms.
• You'll probably have to perform this exercise off the very edge of the bench, possibly
even with your head off it and lifted up a little, as the bar will otherwise shave across
the top of your scalp when using full ROM.

Cable triceps kickbacks
• The cable height should be so that there's tension at elbow lock-out as well as in full
elbow flexion.

19

Overhead triceps extension [cable variant]
• Let a dumbbell or cable lower overhead in a natural pattern and push it back up to
full elbow lock-out with minimal bending of your arm.
• Perform the exercise standing or seated with one arm at a time.

Gagné triceps extension
• The body movement causes eccentric overloading of the long head of the triceps.

Overhead rope triceps extensions
• Try not to move your elbows: keep them in the same position and focus on purely
extending the arms from a full stretch to full elbow lock-out.
• Perform the exercise standing.

20

Pulls
Motorcycle rows [standing one arm version]
• Keep your forearms roughly in line with the cable.
• Your arms should fully straighten in the stretched position with your elbow as high as
possible without losing lat tension; in the contracted position, your hand or the cable
attachment should touch your chest.
• You can perform this exercise seated (as if you're on a motorcycle), but it can also
be performed kneeling or standing with a staggered stance with the foot on your
pulling arm's side placed way forward.

Diagonal rows
• Full range of motion entails letting your arm fully straighten at the top and pulling
your hand into your chest.
• You can lean back as you pull the weight down to match your strength curve to the
resistance.
• Your arm should not go 180° overhead: keep the tension on your lat.

Unilateral pulldowns
• Note the rotation of the forearm.
• Each rep goes from a fully straightened arm overhead until your hand touches your
chest in the bottom position.
• You should lean back slightly as you pull down the weight to keep your forearm in
line with the cable.
• Tempo: explosive concentric, split-second pause with your hand or the cable handle
touching your chest, controlled eccentric, split-second pause with your arm fully
stretched straight overhead.

Supinated grip chin-ups

21

• Use a supinated grip (palms facing yourself) at shoulder width or just outside
shoulder width (whichever feels more comfortable).
• Full ROM requires that you go from a dead hang until your hands or body hit the bar
or your hands.
• Keeping your lower legs crossed behind your back helps to resist the urge to cheat
with hip momentum ('kipping').
• The most convenient way to add resistance to the exercise is by loading a dip belt
around your lower back with the weight dangling in between your legs.

Half chin-up
• With a supinated grip at shoulder width, pull yourself up until your upper arms are
parallel to the floor.

Seated cable row, Arnold style
• Let only your upper back round during the eccentric phase to get a good stretch of
the lats. Your lower back should retain a roughly neutral position, a bit more
conservative than in the video for most people.
• Full range of motion requires going from straight arms to touching the body with the
cable handle or your hands.
• Keep the tension on your muscles: don't let the weight deload in the stretched
position if the cable is short enough for that.
• Tempo: explosively pull the cable into your abdomen and then immediately transition
into a controlled eccentric after which you again immediately reverse the movement
without a pause.

Neutral grip chin-ups
• Use a hammer grip at shoulder width.
• Each rep should go from a dead hang in the bottom until your shoulders hit the
handles at the top.

22

• Keeping your lower legs crossed behind your back helps to resist the urge to cheat
with hip momentum ('kipping').
• Load the exercise with a dip belt dangling in between your legs.

Ring chin-ups [2]
• Each rep goes from a dead hang until your fingers touch your chest in the fully
supinated position (i.e. you rotate your thumbs outwards during the ascent).
• Keeping your lower legs crossed behind your back helps to resist the urge to cheat
with hip momentum ('kipping').
• Tempo: explosive concentric, split second pause with your shoulders against your
hands, controlled eccentric, split second pause in the bottom.

Pull-ups
• Place your hands just outside shoulder width and pull yourself up and literally place
your chin on the bar. Getting your chin over the bar doesn't suffice. You need to
briefly rest the bottom of your chin on the top of the bar. Now lower yourself back
down under control until your arms fully straighten ('dead hang').
• No hip movement ('kipping') is allowed. Keep your lower legs crossed behind your
back.

Ring pull-ups
• Place the rings just outside shoulder width and pull yourself up with a pronated grip
and your elbows going out to the sides until your arms fully flex. Now lower yourself
back down under control until your arms fully straighten ('dead hang').
• No hip movement ('kipping') is allowed. Keep your lower legs crossed behind your
back.

Wide grip lat pulldowns
• Your grip should be just outside shoulder width.
• You can use a V-grip or straight bar if a neutral grip handle is unavailable.

pages.text:
23

• The bar should touch your chest in the bottom position; you'll have to lean back to
achieve this.
• Tempo: explosively pull down the weight into your chest and then control the weight
as it pulls your arms up until your arms fully straighten overhead. Then pause for a
split-second before the next rep.

Close, neutral grip pulldown
• Using a narrow hammer grip, pull down your hands into your chest as you lean back
to keep your forearms in a straight line with the cable.
• Actively use the momentum from leaning back to pull down more weight while
retaining control of the weight to achieve eccentric overloading.
• Tempo: explosively pull down the weight into your chest, pause for a split-second
with your hands touching your chest, control the ascent and pause again for a split-
second with your arms fully stretched out straight overhead.

Supinated grip pulldowns
• Using a standard supinated grip with your palms facing you, pull down your hands
into your chest as you lean back to keep your forearms in a straight line with the
cable.
• Actively use the momentum from leaning back to pull down more weight while
retaining control of the weight to achieve eccentric overloading (more than in the
video is fine).
• Tempo: explosively pull down the weight into your chest, pause for a split-second
with your hands touching your chest, control the ascent and pause again for a split-
second with your arms fully stretched out straight overhead.

Barbell bent-over row
• Keep your hips and back in the bottom position of a Romanian deadlift. Your spine
and pelvis should stay in neutral alignment. Do not allow your spine to flex visibly

24

and keep your hips firmly in position during the entire set. This is not a lower body
exercise.
• The rowing motion is effectively the reverse of the barbell bench press. Use a similar
grip width as you use for that.

25

Upper back exercises
Face-pulls
• Note the underhand grip.
• For a maximal stretch, your arms should fully straighten and your shoulder blades
should be dragged forward a bit by the weight (scapular protraction).
• In the fully contracted position your hands should be vertically in line with your
shoulders.

Dumbbell high rows
• Hold onto a rack/pole or lean on a bench with your free hand.
• With your elbow and shoulder way out to the side, pull your elbow up as far as
possible. Don't pull it towards your body. Pull it all the way out to the side.
• Think of pulling with your elbows, not your hands. Your forearms basically just
function like hooks during this exercise.
• Your torso should stay rigidly in position during the whole set. Your back should stay
flat/horizontal. You're performing a controlled rowing motion, not starting up a
lawnmower.
• Your elbow should go behind your body in the top position to achieve full range of
motion.
• Let your arm fully straighten in the bottom position and let the weight pull your
shoulder blade forward, but do not let your spine round.

Landmine high rows
• With your elbow and shoulder way out to the side, pull your elbow up as far as
possible. Don't pull it towards your body. Pull it all the way out to the side.
• Think of pulling with your elbows, not your hands. Your forearms basically just
function like hooks during this exercise.

26

• Your torso should stay rigidly in position during the whole set. Your back should stay
flat/horizontal. You're performing a controlled rowing motion, not starting up a
lawnmower.
• Your elbow should go behind your body in the top position to achieve full range of
motion.
• Let your arm fully straighten in the bottom position and let the weight pull your
shoulder blade forward, but do not let your spine round.

Cross-over rows
• To achieve full ROM, your elbows have to end up behind your body.

Wide barbell row
• With a wide grip, heave the barbell into your chest, as high up your body as
possible.
• Your grip width should be as wide as possible while still being able to touch your
chest with the bar without momentum.
• Keep your spine and pelvis in a neutral position: you should keep them in the bottom
position of a Romanian deadlift.
• Your torso should not move during the exercise.

27

• In the bottom position, your arms should fully straighten and your shoulders should
come down (scapular protraction) for full scapular range of motion.

Wide cable row
• With a wide neutral or pronated grip, heave the bar into your chest, as high up your
body as possible.
• Your elbows should end up behind your torso.
• Your grip width should be as wide as possible while still being able to touch your
torso with the bar.
• The cable should stay horizontal.
• Your torso should stay vertical.
• In the stretched position, your arms should fully straighten and your shoulders
should come forward for full scapular range of motion.

28

Shoulder-pulls
• With the longest rope in the gym, using an overhand grip (standard), heave each rep
until your shoulders and elbows are behind your body. Then control the weight as it
straightens your arms.
• Perform the exercise kneeling or on a seated cable row station.
• This is a strict shoulder-pull, not a row or a face-pull, meaning your hands and
elbows should stay at shoulder height (horizontal). If you don't have a long rope
though, the exercise will look more like a face-pull.

Bayesian reverse fly
• This is like a regular bent-over/rear delt cable raise with the modification that your
shoulder is in an internally rotated position to emphasize the rear delts. You achieve
this by using a cable rope attachment.
• Keep your arm (almost) fully extended: don't allow any bending of the arm to occur
during the movement. It's a fly, not a row.
• All movement should come from the shoulder: there should be minimal spinal
rotation and hip movement.
• Full range of motion is achieved by letting your upper arm be pulled into your body
beyond when its perpendicular to the floor in the bottom position (so that your arm is
squeezing your pecs together); at the top, your elbow should reach at least shoulder
height.
• If you experience a pronounced sticking point at the top, step back further away from
the cable tower to get relatively more resistance in the bottom and less at the top.

Suspended high rows

Dumbbell reverse fly

Side lying reverse flys
• Let the dumbbell come down as far as possible to get a maximum stretch.

29

• You can move the dumbbell all the way up vertically, but there's little muscle tension
at the top, so it's also fine to only go about two-thirds of the way up.

30

Shrugs
Monkey shrugs

Overhead shrugs
• Try to keep your arms straight, but some natural bending is fine if it helps you
engage your traps.
• Control the descent: don't let the weight free-fall down.
• Use a grip outside shoulder width: you can go as wide as you like in principle.
• Shrug up your shoulders as high as possible: don't skimp on the inherently short
range of motion.

Wide cable shrugs
• The further the pulleys are apart, the better, as you want your shoulders to be in
abduction.
• You might need to wear powerlifting straps to avoid your grip limiting your
performance.
• Keep your arms straight during the exercise. It's a shrug, not an upright row.
• Keep your neck slightly flexed (look down a bit) and bend over forwards to avoid
active insufficiency of the upper trapezius.
• Make sure your shoulder blades go all the way down and rise all the way up during
the exercise: maximize your range of motion.

Wide barbell shrugs
• You might need to wear powerlifting straps to avoid your grip limiting your
performance.
• Keep your arms straight during the exercise. It's a shrug, not an upright row.
• Keep your neck slightly flexed (look down a bit) and bend over forwards to avoid
active insufficiency of the upper trapezius.

31

• Make sure your shoulder blades go all the way down and rise all the way up during
the exercise: maximize your range of motion.
Horizontal shrugs
• This exercise often works particularly well in a chest-supported row machine. You
can also create the chest-support yourself with an upright bench.
• Think of pinching a pencil in between your shoulder blades during the contraction
and then let your shoulder blades drift all the way forward into a maximum stretch.
• Keep your arms straight and your back upright. Practically all movement should
come from the shoulder blades.

32

Lat exercises
Decline pull-overs with cable / dumbbell / barbell
• If possible, position the bench at a 15-45° decline to increase range of motion. Use
as much decline as possible without limiting range of motion in the bottom, i.e. the
weight should not touch the floor. If you use a cable, which is ideal, use the lowest
pulley and pull/push the cable down as far as possible with straight arms.

• You can either lie on the bench with your whole body or just with your upper back.
• Keep the tension on your lats in the bottom position and avoid shoulder
hyperextension.

Kayak lat prayer
• If you're not too strong yet to move weights over about half your bodyweight, you
should be able to perform this exercise standing. Otherwise, you may need to
perform the exercise on your knees.
• Lean into the weight until your arms are almost fully extended (less than 180° ROM):
keep the tension on your lats, not your connective tissue.
• With your arms kept (nearly) straight, pull the bar into your hip, starting with your
weakest side. After you've completed all reps with your weaker side, rest for a
moment and then perform the same number of reps with your stronger side.

Lat prayers
• Position yourself on your knees or standing in front of a high cable pulley with a
V- or W-grip or rope attachment.
headings: Decline pull-overs with cable / dumbbell / barbell

pages.text:
33

• Lean forward into the weight and let your arms almost but not fully extend (less
than 180° ROM: aim for 120-170°): keep the tension on your lats, not your
connective tissue.
• With straight arms, pull the bar/rope into your lap while leaning backwards. It's a
rhythmic motion.
• In the bottom position, your elbows should be in line with your torso, effectively in
anatomical position.
• Use your body movement to get a good stretch in the top position (torso more
horizontal) and a good peak contraction in the bottom position (torso more
vertical). If you don't get a good stretch, lean over further forwards during the
ascent. Kneeling down helps you get a good stretch too. If you don't get good
peak contraction, lean more backwards during the descent, or step back a bit
further from the cable tower.

34

Biceps isolation exercises
Reverse grip barbell curl
• Use a shoulder width or slightly wider pronated grip ('reverse grip'). Use a thumbless
grip if it's more comfortable for your wrists or elbows.
• Lean forwards a little bit (~15° at the hip) or push your elbows forward in the bottom
position to achieve a full biceps stretch.
• Keep your elbows at your sides to maintain tension throughout the full range of
motion: do not let your elbows travel forward.
• Do not use any body momentum.

Drag curls
• Use a barbell or EZ-bar with a shoulder-width or slightly wider grip and quite literally
drag the barbell up across your body to full biceps flexion.
• Bend over slightly (15°) or push your elbows forward a little in the bottom position to
keep the tension on your biceps in the stretched position.
• Do not let your elbows come forward much during the exercise: keep the tension on
your biceps in the top position.
• Do not use any body momentum.

Barbell curl
• Use a shoulder width or slightly wider supinated grip.
• Lean forwards a little bit (~15° at the hip) or push your elbows forward at the bottom
to achieve a full biceps stretch.
• Keep your elbows at your sides to maintain tension throughout the full range of
motion: do not let your elbows travel forward.
• Use an EZ-bar if this is more comfortable for your wrists or elbows.

Bayesian curl [2, 3]

35

• Lean forward as you curl up the weight to get a good peak biceps contraction. Lean
backward as you let the weight come down under control to get a good biceps
stretch in the bottom (biomechanical resistance curve accommodation).
• The cable pulley should not touch your forearm at any point. If it does, you're either
moving your elbow forward too much or you're not leaning forward enough with your
torso.
• If you're interested in the detailed rationale for this exercise, see this article or this
video.

Bayesian hammer curl
• Like the regular Bayesian curl but with a hammer/neutral grip (cable rope
attachment).

Offset incline dumbbell curls
• Note the rotation off the forearm: supinate your wrist as you curl up the weight.
• For the offset grip, your thumbs should be high up on the handle, touching the plates
of the dumbbell, not in the middle as with a conventional grip.
• Perform the exercise with one arm and one dumbbell at a time.
• Incline the bench at 60-45° instead of perfectly vertically upright (90°) to get a
maximum stretch in your biceps in the bottom position.

Zottman curls
• Note the rotation of the forearm: this is done to achieve eccentric overloading.
• This exercise has to be performed with control: explosively curl up the dumbbells,
pronate your forearms and then lower the dumbbells carefully to a full stretch.

Standing alternating dumbbell curl

Hammer concentration curls

36

• Lean away from the dumbbell a bit more than in the video so that your arm remains
slightly diagonal in the bottom position and you retain muscle tension.
Leaning concentration curls (supinated)

Unilateral dumbbell curl

Dumbbell preacher curls
• Slowly let your arm fully straighten while maintaining biceps tension.
• If you don't have a preacher curl bench, you can use an incline bench.

37

Ab work
Abdominal crunch
• This is the classic crunch exercise, performed on a (bosu) ball for maximum range of
motion. A half bosu ball is most convenient for stability.
• All movement should come from the spine. It is not a sit-up, so there should be no
hip movement. Think of crunching your pelvis and your chest together. The
movement is very short compared to a sit-up.
• You can load the exercise with a plate or dumbbell behind your neck, but do not pull
on your neck with your hands. If you cannot resist pulling on your neck, hold a
dumbbell high up on your chest, up against and in line with your collar bone. When
you become too strong to use a dumbbell, you can use a rope cable pulley for
additional resistance.

Reverse crunch
• If you're not strong enough to keep your legs straight, keep your knees bent.
• In the bottom position, get your legs down as far as they can go to get a good
stretch.
• At the top, crunch up your abs to maximally involve the abs. You can go into a full
dragon flag position as in the video, if you're strong enough, or just crunch up from
the lower abs and keep your back on the bench.
• Perform this exercise with control, not explosively, to protect your spine.
• You can load the exercise with ankle weights or a dumbbell or medicine ball in
between your ankles or thighs. If you have trouble loading the exercise heavily
headings: motion. A half bosu ball is most convenient for stability.

pages.text:
38

enough, increase the incline of the bench. If the exercise is too hard even without
weight, set the bench at a decline or perform the exercise on the floor. The greater
the incline, the more difficult the exercise. When you run out of progression options
again, you can perform the exercise with your hips hoovering in the air below/after
the bench instead of with your butt on the bench. This is much harder.

39

Neck exercises
Traditional strength training, including deadlifts, shrugs and overhead presses,
stimulates minimal neck muscle hypertrophy or neck extension strength. Neck-specific
exercises are needed in case neck hypertrophy and strengthening are desired.

A neck harness makes all neck exercises considerably more comfortable and easier to
microload.

Neck bridges

Neck extensions

Neck flexion with a cable / plate

Lateral neck flexion with plate
headings: Neck flexion with a cable / plate

pages.text:
40

Forearm exercises
Dumbbell wrist curls
• Keep the tension on your muscles in the bottom position. Get a good stretch, but
don't let the weight hang into your connective tissues unresisted.

Dumbbell wrist extensions
• Keep the tension on your muscles in the bottom position. Get a good stretch, but
don't let the weight hang into your connective tissues unresisted.

Wrist pronation (best performed with an adjustable dumbbell loaded only on one side)
• Keep the tension on your muscles in the bottom position. Get a good stretch, but
don't let the weight hang into your connective tissues unresisted.

Wrist supination (best performed with an adjustable dumbbell loaded only on one side)
• Keep the tension on your muscles in the bottom position. Get a good stretch, but
don't let the weight hang into your connective tissues unresisted.
headings: Wrist pronation (best performed with an adjustable dumbbell loaded only on one side)
Wrist supination (best performed with an adjustable dumbbell loaded only on one side)

pages.text:
41

Lower body

Squat type movements
Staggered stance squat
• This is in all aspects a squat and it can be performed with a high, low or front squat
bar position. The only difference is that the rear foot should be on its toes, not on its
heel/midfoot like during most squats.

Deficit deadlift
• This is a regular deadlift performed from a platform/elevation/plates to increase
range of motion. The deficit/elevation should be as large as possible while still
allowing you to retain your natural spinal arch in the bottom position. If you cannot
practically implement a maximal deficit, use a Snatch grip.
• At no point during the exercise should any visible back rounding (spinal flexion)
occur. Your lower back should remain flat or, ideally, even retain its natural arch.
• Lock out your pelvis at the top of each rep by contracting your glutes. Then lower the
weight under control by pushing your hips back until they can't go back any further,
exactly as if it was a Romanian deadlift, before you drop the weight. The last few
inches of the negative put your spine at great risk of rounding (lumbar spinal flexion),
so dropping the weight and resetting it between every rep is safer and a good way to
perform eccentrically overloaded RDLs.
• You can use any training gear you're allowed to use in competition.

Unilateral leg press / 45° variant
• Pick a foot position that allows you to use maximum range of motion. That means
your knee should get as close to your chest as possible and your knee should bend
as much as possible.

42

• Prevent your lower back from flexing (rounding) and your pelvis from tucking under
(posterior pelvic tilt) excessively, just like during squats, to protect your spine and
sacroiliac joint. However, you can be much more liberal with this exercise due to the
lack of spinal loading and lower weights used with one-legged exercises.
• Straighten your leg, but do not hyperextend your knee.
• Tempo: explosive concentric, take one quick breath, controlled eccentric
transitioning without pause into the next concentric.

Leg press
• Pick a foot position that allows you to use maximum range of motion (ROM). That
means your knee should get as close to your chest as possible and your knee
should bend as much as possible. (Total ROM = knee flexion + hip flexion.)
• You can do this exercise in a leg press where you push yourself away from the
platform (closed kinetic chain) or a leg press where you push the sled away from
yourself. If you have both, try both and see which feels best over the largest possible
ROM.
• Prevent your lower back from flexing (rounding) and your pelvis from tucking under
(posterior pelvic tilt), just like during squats, to protect your spine and sacroiliac joint.
Your lumbopelvic complex should stay neutral throughout the entire set.
• Straighten your legs at the top, but do not hyperextend your knees.
• Tempo: explosive concentric, take one quick breath, controlled eccentric
transitioning without pause into the next concentric.

Pistols
• To make it easier to balance yourself, hold a plate or dumbbell in your hands. It will
function as a counterbalance.
• Significantly more spinal flexion than with other squats is acceptable and often
required here.

43

• If you have trouble keeping your non-working leg up, even though that's part of the
exercise, you can do the exercise off a step or box to make this easier.
• Resistance can be added to the exercise by increasing the weight in your hands or
wearing a weighted vest. A backpack with weights in it can work too but generally it
will pull you off balance when the weight becomes just as heavy as what you're
holding in your hands.

Reverse lunge from deficit
• Step back from a box or step to get in the deepest possible squat position you can
achieve without having your back round over (spinal flexion) or your pelvis tuck
under (posterior pelvic tilt). You should be able to go significantly below parallel,
probably 'ass-to-grass'.
• If you're too strong to hold the dumbbells, do the exercise with a barbell on your
back in either back or front squat position (preferably in a safety rack). A Smith
machine can also be used to make the exercise easier and safer.
• Start the first work set with your weaker leg, rest, and then perform the same
number of reps with your stronger leg.

Low bar squats [2]
• Use a thumbless, overhand grip.

• Position your feet at or just outside of shoulder width pointing outwards at 5-45°.

44

• Squat down as deep as possible while
A) maintaining a neutral or anteriorly tilted pelvis,
B) keeping your knees tracked over your toes and
C) keeping the bar directly vertically above the midfoot.
• It helps to breathe in deeply through your abdomen before any difficult rep and keep
the air there until you're past the sticking point (Valsalva maneuver). If you do it right,
it looks as if you become 6 months pregnant and your chest doesn't move at all.
• Between each rep, breathe a fixed number of times: one deep breath works best for
most people, depending on their conditioning. Be sure that you don't start resting
longer in between reps as you get fatigued.
• Do not use any training gear (e.g. wraps or belt) and wear minimalist footwear or go
barefoot until we've established proper technique.

Front squat [2, 3, cross-over/bodybuilding grip]
• Think of sitting down in between your legs, not folding over.
• If your flexibility allows it, use an Olympic Clean grip. It's fine if at first you can only
get a few fingers under the bar with wide grip and need to stretch your wrists
beforehand.

If you really dislike the Olympic grip, you can use a bodybuilder/cross-over grip.

45

• If you're using an Olympic grip, it can help to think of leading with the elbows:
actively push them up. Leading with the elbows will help prevent rounding of the
spine, allow you to stay more upright and move more weight. It's normal to feel that
this becomes difficult as you approach muscle failure.
• Position your feet just at or outside of shoulder width pointing outwards at 5-45°.
• Go as deep as possible while A) maintaining a neutral or anteriorly tilted pelvis, B)
keeping your knees tracked over your toes and C) keeping the bar directly vertical
above the midfoot. Most people can go below parallel.
• It helps to breathe in deeply through your belly (Valsalva maneuver) before you go
down each rep to tighten your core until you're past the sticking point. After the
sticking point, at the top, can breathe again.
• Between each rep, breathe a fixed number of times: 1-2 breaths works best for most
people, depending on their conditioning. Be sure that you don't start resting longer in
between reps as you get fatigued.
• Do not use any training gear (e.g. a belt or wraps) and preferably wear minimalist
footwear or go barefoot until we've established proper technique.

Goblet squats
• Position your feet at or just outside of shoulder width pointing outwards at 5-45°.
• Squat down as deep as possible while
A) maintaining a neutral or anteriorly tilted pelvis,
B) keeping your knees tracked over your toes and
C) keeping the dumbbell directly vertically above the midfoot.
• It helps to breathe in deeply through your abdomen before any difficult rep and keep
the air there until you're past the sticking point (Valsalva maneuver). If you do it right,
it looks as if you become 6 months pregnant and your chest doesn't move at all.
• Between each rep, breathe a fixed number of times: one deep breath works best for
most people, depending on their conditioning. Be sure that you don't start resting
longer in between reps as you get fatigued.

46

High bar squats [2, 3, 4, 5]
• Position your feet at or just outside of shoulder width pointing outwards at 5-45°.
• Squat down as deep as possible while
A) maintaining a neutral or anteriorly tilted pelvis,
B) keeping your knees tracked over your toes and
C) keeping the bar directly vertically above the midfoot.
• It helps to breathe in deeply through your abdomen before any difficult rep and keep
the air there until you're past the sticking point (Valsalva maneuver). If you do it right,
it looks as if you become 6 months pregnant and your chest doesn't move at all.
• Between each rep, breathe a fixed number of times: one deep breath works best for
most people, depending on their conditioning. Be sure that you don't start resting
longer in between reps as you get fatigued.
• Do not use any training gear (e.g. wraps or belt) and wear minimalist footwear or go
barefoot until we've established proper technique.

Bulgarian split squat from deficit [side angle]
• Hold a dumbbell in each hand, or use a barbell with either a front or back squat bar
placement, if you prefer or need to because you don't have access to sufficiently
heavy dumbbells. If you use dumbbells, when increasing the weight, you can first
only increase the weight of the dumbbell in your contralateral hand (the hand on the
other side of your working leg, e.g. if you're standing on your left leg, you increase
the weight in your right hand) before you also increase the ipsilateral dumbbell's
weight.
• The back foot should lie flat on a bench, leg extension pad or other elevation. The
front foot should be placed on a low box or step.
• Keep the weight over your front foot.
• Keep your rear leg's knee roughly under your hips to avoid straining or
overstretching your rear leg's hip flexors. This is an example of overstretching the
hip flexors, though it is otherwise excellent technique.
headings: Bulgarian split squat from deficit [side angle]

pages.text:
47

• If you experience 'stability problems', that's normal. The ability to produce force
during a given movement pattern is strength, so what you experience as a lack of
stability is basically a lack of strength in the target musculature. It will get much
better over time.
If using free weights is too hardcore for you (it's a brutal exercise after all), you can
also perform this exercise in a Smith machine, ideally with your front foot directly
below the bar on a step for maximum ROM.
• Feel free to rest in between sets of both legs. Your rear leg still contributes a
significant amount of force (~25%) during split squats.

Step-ups
• Keep the toes of your back leg curled up. This forces you to rely on the front leg to
push yourself up and to control the descent. Act as if you're stepping down on a
brittle plate of glass: you need to gently step down on it and only tap the floor, not
come falling down into it with your full weight.
• When increasing the weight, you can first only increase the weight of the dumbbell in
your contralateral hand (the hand on the other side of your working leg, e.g. if you're
standing on your left leg, you increase the weight in your right hand) before you also
increase the ipsilateral dumbbell's weight.
• The step should be as high as possible to maximize range of motion (ROM). Ideally,
the start position resembles a deep squat position for your front leg.

48

Quad isolation exercises
Terminal knee extension (TKE)
• You can increase the resistance by using a stronger band, stepping further away
from the band, double-looping the band around your leg or leaning more of your
bodyweight onto your knee. Alternatively, if you have a suitable strap, you can
perform them in front of a cable pulley.
• Keep your rear leg on its toes about 1 foot behind your front foot.
• Think of the front leg's movement as a partial squat with more aggressive knee
extension (but not full knee lock-out).
• A more difficult version is the Spanish squat, which is the same exercise, except that
you bend down all the way into a full squat.

Machine Sissy squats
• Go down as deep as possible. You should be able to go fully 'ass-to-grass'.
• Maintain a consistent torso position. If you're strong enough, keep your torso
vertical.
• You can add load by holding a dumbbell in your hands in a goblet position.

Rope-assisted sissy squats
• Go down as deep as possible. You should be able to go fully 'ass-to-grass'.
• Maintain a consistent torso position. If you're strong enough, keep your hips fully
extended (locked out) with a glute contraction.
• Instead of having a belt with a rope around your waist, you can also hold on to a
rope attachment, like you'd use for pushdowns from a cable pulley around hip
height, loaded to around 50% of bodyweight (or higher if you need more assistance).

Spanish squats

49

The exercise can be made harder by using stronger bands, double-looping the bands or
standing further away from the band attachment point.

Reverse Nordic ham curls
• This is a quadriceps movement, effectively a sitting Sissy squat. You'll probably
experience a major quad stretch.
• You can add load by holding a dumbbell up high against your collar bone.
• If you're not strong enough to do these with flat feet, you can perform them up on
your toes.

Unilateral leg extension
• Perform with one leg at a time.
• Straighten your legs at the top, but don't fully lock out your knees.
• Lower the pad as far down as possible without letting the weight come to a dead
stop, i.e. maintain muscle tension.
• Position the machine so as to maximize range of motion. The further you need to
bend your knee in the bottom position, the better.
• If seated, lean back around 45° to increase tension on the rectus femoris.

Toe squats
• Perform squats on your tippy toes up until your knees are about 90° bent. Keep the
tension on your quads. Don't go all the way up. Then sink down slowly into an ass-
to-grass full squats before squatting back up.
• You'll generally need to hold on to something in front of you for balance. It can be
anything. It doesn't have to be a band above you like in the video.

50

Hip hinges
Staggered stance Goodmorning
• This is in all aspects a Goodmorning. The only difference is that the rear foot should
be on its toes, not on its heel/midfoot like during most squats.
• Use a low bar position.

Goodmorning
• With a barbell on your back, using a low bar position, push your hips back as far as
possible while maintaining the natural arch in your back. Once you reach the fully
stretched position, reverse the movement.
• Do not allow your back to noticeably round over (spinal flexion) during any part of
the exercise.

Sumo Goodmorning
• With a barbell on your back, using a low bar position, push your hips back as far as
possible while maintaining the natural arch in your back. Once you reach the fully
stretched position, reverse the movement.
• Do not allow your back to noticeably round over (spinal flexion) during any part of
the exercise.

Pull-throughs
• Do not let your lower back round over at any point during the exercise. This is
basically a Romanian deadlift performed with a cable, so there should be no lumbar
spinal flexion. All movement should come from the hips.
• Push your hips back as far as possible. Then push the hips forward while contracting
your glutes until complete hip lock-out.
• When you get to non-novice level weights that pull you back, you'll have to lean way
into the cable so that you stand diagonally in the to position ( like / instead of | ).

51

Reverse hyperextension
• Keep your pelvis neutral or even tilted anteriorly (hips towards the ceiling) in the
bottom position. From there, contract your hams and glutes to lift the weight up
explosively. At the top, contract your glutes to posteriorly tilt your pelvis and push
your hips into the bench, or whatever you're lying on.
• It generally helps to think of the movement as pushing your hips into the bench
rather than thinking of bringing your feet up.
• If your gym doesn't have a true reverse hyper machine, you can improvise by using
a high bench/platform, back extension or laying over an exercise ball. Resistance
can be added with a band around or dumbbell between your ankles.

Cable hip extension
• If possible, stand on a weight plate so that you can keep your leg perfectly straight.
It's a hip extension movement, not a kickback.

45° Hip extension
• Often erroneously known as back- or hyperextensions, all movement should come
from the hips while you maintain a neutral spine. Your pelvis should be anteriorly
tilted in the bottom position (push your hips upwards towards the ceiling) and
posteriorly (push your hips forward/downward and contract your glutes) in the top
position.
Note the difference with true back (hyper) extensions.
• Hold a dumbbell horizontally high up against your chest, close to your neck, so that it
touches both of your collar bones. Make sure you always hold the weight in the
same manner. The position of the weight greatly affects the leverage of the weight
and thereby the difficulty of the exercise.
• Your knees should be free to move, so the padding should start just above them.
• When your legs become too strong for your upper body to hold onto enough weight
to sufficiently load the exercise, perform the exercise unilaterally: with one leg free in
the air.

52

Romanian deadlift [2]
• This movement is a pure hip hinge. Position your feet directly below your hips,
pointing straight forwards. Do not think of getting the bar down. Instead, simply push
your butt back as far as possible, while keeping your spine and pelvis in their neutral
position. Then explode back up until your hips lock out fully. Don't think about how
low the bar goes. Think only about your hips and back.
• There's no need to retract your shoulder blades, but don't let them drift forwards
either.
• Keep the bar close. It's common to have the bar literally brush up against your legs
throughout much of the movement.
• Straps are allowed and even recommended if you otherwise can't hold the bar with a
double overhand grip. See this video for how to use powerlifting straps or this one.
Any brand designed for powerlifting should be fine, e.g. Harbinger, Rogue, EliteFTS.
• To learn how to perform a pure hip hinge, perform some RDLs with a broomstick
held against your back. Only 2 parts of your spine should touch the broomstick at
any point during the set: your upper back and your tailbone area. If you feel your
lower back touching the broomstick in the bottom, that means undesirable spinal
flexion.

53

Unilateral Romanian deadlift (one-legged)
• Examples (all fine: pick whichever variation you prefer):
Staggered stance (most people aren't this flexible)
Traditional execution (hit mute before clicking)
Supported stance
• The trailing leg may be placed on a bench, on its toes or in the air, as long as you're
consistent. You can hold whatever implement you prefer. Most people prefer a
staggered stance while holding a barbell.
• Focus on full hip lock-out and glute activation (posterior tilted pelvis) at the top and a
maximum hamstring stretch at the bottom while keeping your spine in its natural arc
(no spinal flexion).
• Powerlifting straps are allowed, but use them only if needed to maintain a double
overhand grip. Don't use a mixed grip, unless you're a Powerlifter and straps aren't
allowed in your competitions.

Powerlifting conventional deadlift [2]; Sumo style [2]
• Take a big breath of air through your belly before you start the pull off the floor and
keep it there until you lock out the bar (Valsalva maneuver).
• The bar should lie over the middle of your feet so that it's just in front of your shins or
touching your shins. Your weight should rest on your heels.
• Your arms should function purely as hooks, hanging down straight with your
shoulders slightly in front of the bar (arms close to vertical).
• Use a double overhand grip in the rare case your grip is strong enough; most people
will need to use a mixed grip with chalk, assuming straps are not allowed in your
target competition.
• Your hips should be set up in between a squat and Romanian deadlift position,
however you're strongest. If your hips shoot up when you start the pull no matter
what you do, they were probably too low. If you can't engage your quads, they're
probably too high. Your torso and hips should rise at roughly the same speed as you
stand up with the bar in your hands.

54

• Your upper back can be slightly or even significantly rounded over, but try to keep
your lower back flat. Your spine should not move much during the lift, especially not
your lower back. Your head position doesn't inherently matter: keep it relatively
neutral in a way that feels comfortable.
• Pull yourself tight into the bar before you apply maximum effort. You may be able to
do this rapidly if you're explosive, but it's also fine to already pull the slack out of the
bar before you go for the pull off the floor.
• Push through your heels.
• Keep the bar close during your pull. It's fine if it brushes over your shins and thighs.
• Lock out the bar by pushing your hips forward with a glute contraction.
• Use all training gear you're allowed to use in your next planned competition.

55

Leg curls
Nordic ham curl
• Have someone hold your ankles or place them securely under a loaded barbell or a
lat pulldown attachment (shins on seat, ankles under the pads).
• Place your knees on a Bosu-ball or thick Airex/rubber/foam mat on the floor.
• Lower your body towards the floor in a controlled tempo and reverse just before you
face plant.
• Your hips should stay in neutral position or locked-out: you should be able to draw a
straight line from your knees to your shoulders throughout the entire set.
• If you're not strong enough for unassisted reps (it's an elite level exercise), you can
use push-up momentum in the bottom position or perform the exercise with band
assistance.

Seated leg curl
• Set up the machine to get as much knee flexion range of motion as possible. And
always set up the machine in the same way so that you can reliably track your
progress.
• Keep your toes pointed towards the ceiling (dorsiflexion) to let your calves (gastrocs)
aid in the movement.
• If your sticking point is in the bottom position, lean forward in your seat. If the
hardest part of the exercise is in the top position, lean back in your seat. Keep your
butt on the seat at all times.
• If possible, perform the exercise unilaterally, i.e. with one leg at a time.

Standing leg curl
• Set up the machine to maximize ROM. Your leg should fully straighten (but not
hyperextend) in the bottom position. At the top the pad should bump against your
hamstring.
headings: lat pulldown attachment (shins on seat, ankles under the pads).
• Place your knees on a Bosu-ball or thick Airex/rubber/foam mat on the floor.

pages.text:
56

• Curl up your toes towards your knees (dorsiflexion) to let your gastrocs aid in the
movement.
• Lean over forward to put your hamstrings in a more stretched position and increase
strength.

Walk-outs
• Walking out until both legs are fully straightened and then stepping back to the start
position equals one repetition.
• You can load the exercise with a dumbbell, plates or even a barbell in your lap,
though you may have to use small plates to avoid the plates touching the floor and
limiting your range of motion. You can also advance to the harder variant by
keeping your hips locked out with a glute contraction throughout the entire set.

Gliding leg curl
• You can load this exercise with weights in a backpack if you don't have a weighted
vest or chains. Even without loading, however, you can progress a lot with just
bodyweight. Start by performing the exercise without your hips kept low and the
bench positioned relatively close to the squat rack. Then you can increase exercise
difficulty in the following ways in order of difficulty:
▪ Position the bench further away from the squat rack.
▪ Extend your hips and lock out your pelvis by contracting your glutes before
performing the leg curl, as shown in the video.
▪ Perform the exercise with one leg and repeat the above cycle.

Suspended leg curl
• Start with your legs fully extended (hips locked out fully with a glute contraction) and
the middle soles of your feet in a straight hanging suspender (e.g. TRX) just above
floor level. Now flex your knees, trying to kick your butt with your ankles. You'll likely
feel it in your hamstrings and calves.

57

• No part of your body other than your upper back should be in contact with the floor
at any point during the set.
• You can add load by placing weight plates on your abdomen, as low as possible
without interfering with your range of motion.
When this is no longer practically possible, you can increase the exercise difficulty in
the following ways in order of difficulty.
- Lower the suspenders (if possible without touching the floor).
- Move yourself further away from the suspender's anchor point so that the
suspender is hanging down diagonally in the bottom position.
- Keep your hips extended (glutes contracted) throughout the whole set. Unlike what
the video shows, keep the suspenders just above the floor and don't touch the floor
with any part of your body other than your upper back. The exercise will feel almost
like a hip thrust with your hips kept in lock-out. This variation is much harder than the
above ones.
- Unilateral execution: perform the exercise with only one leg in a suspender and the
other leg floating in the air. This variation requires a highly advanced strength level.

Unilateral lying leg curl
• Your leg should fully straighten in the bottom position and the pad should hit your
legs/butt in the top position of every rep, assuming the machine allows this.
• Keep your ankles in dorsiflexion (toes up towards knees/calves stretched) to
maximize mechanical tension in the gastrocnemius of the calves.

Glute-ham raise [2]
• This exercise consists of a glute raise (hip extension) followed by a ham raise (knee
flexion). You should use the momentum generated by the initial hip extension to aid
in the subsequent leg curl movement.
• Your pelvis and spine should stay in a neutral position throughout the entire
movement.

58

Cable lying leg curls
• Perform with one leg at a time for optimal results (unlike the above video).
• Your leg should fully straighten in the bottom position to stretch the hamstrings.
Then contract the hamstrings until full knee flexion.
• Keep your ankles in dorsiflexion (toes up towards knees/calves stretched) to
maximize mechanical tension in the gastrocnemius of the calves.
• Keep your butt down. Think of draping yourself
• If the cable is not below the ankle in the bottom position, lower the cable or perform
the exercise on a slightly incline bench to prop up your heels. We want high tension
in the stretched position.

59

Erector spinae exercises
Back extensions

Supermans

60

Calf exercises
Barbell standing calf raise against the rack
• You should reach a full calf stretch in the bottom position and reach full ankle lock-
out at the top.

Straight-leg calf raise
• This exercise can be performed in a Smith machine, leg press or a straight-leg calf
raise machine.
• You should reach a full calf stretch in the bottom position - while retaining muscle
tension - and you should reach full ankle lock-out at the top.
• Your legs should be close to fully extended to maximize the stretch in the bottom
position. Your knees should not bend during any part of the movement.

Calf jumps
• Find a closed chain calf raise, leg press (ideally one where you push your body
away and your feet remain in the same position) or a Smith machine and perform a
calf raise so explosively that you effectively jump without losing contact with the
machine with either your feet or your shoulders.
• The bottom and top positions are exactly the same as during normal calf raises, but
you 'cheat' yourself up to lock-out. So you should reach a full stretch in the bottom
position and reach full ankle lock-out at the top.
• A major benefit of the exercise is that you get eccentric overloading due to your
momentum aiding the concentric (upward movement) but not the eccentric
(downward movement) contraction. To achieve this, you must control the eccentric
and lower yourself with (nearly) straight legs. Only allow your knees to travel forward
during the concentric as part of the 'jumping' motion. The eccentric phase thus looks
exactly like that of a strict straight-leg calf raise, but the concentric phase looks like a
jumping/cheating calf raise.
headings: Barbell standing calf raise against the rack
• This exercise can be performed in a Smith machine, leg press or a straight-leg calf

pages.text:
61

Unilateral calf raise [2]
• Load the exercise with a dumbbell in your working leg's hand and hold onto
something at shoulder height.
• Don't press down on your arm to push yourself up.
• You should reach a full calf stretch in the bottom position and reach full ankle lock-
out at the top.
• When your calves become too strong to hold onto the dumbbell, you can start
performing unilateral donkey calf raises with a dip belt or perform the exercise in a
Smith machine, calf raise or leg press.

Seated calf raise

• Lean forwards in the bottom position to overload the calf stretch. Then lean back as
you press up the weight with your calves.
• You should reach a full stretch in the bottom position and reach full ankle lock-out at
the top.
• If you don't have access to any seated (knees bent) calf raise machine, you can
improvise by doing the exercise in the Smith machine or under a leg extension.

62

Glute exercises
Glute kickbacks
• This exercise can be performed with various machines, including a pendulum
kickback or one of various glute kickback machines.
• All movement should come from the hip: do not hyperextend your lumbar spine.
Think of leaning over forward. If your torso is horizontal, your upper leg should not
come up much higher than horizontal.
• Without a dedicated machine, you may also be able to perform this exercise below a
Smith machine or a lying leg curl machine:

Machine hip abductions
• If possible, put foam pads against the knee pads to maximize the range of motion.

Cable lying hip abductions
• Perform the exercise lying on your side with your leg draping off a bench to increase
the range of motion.
• The cable should ideally be positioned directly below the foot so that it's still pulling
your foot down in the bottom position.

Dumbbell lying hip abduction
• Perform the exercise lying on your side with your leg draping off a bench to increase
the range of motion.

63

Cable standing hip abduction
• Get a good stretch at the bottom by letting your moving leg cross over the standing
leg in the bottom position.
• Try to keep your pelvis and torso rigid: move only your leg.
• The standing leg will also be isometrically performing hip abduction during the
exercise, so make sure to rest a bit in between sets for the other leg.

Side plank hip abductions
• The lower leg will be doing most of the work by pushing you up into a side plank via
active hip abduction. The top leg's hip abduction will further increase the demands
placed on the bottom leg.
• Start with your weaker side and rest at least a minute before repeating the same
number of reps with your stronger side, as both legs are involved each set.
• This is an advanced exercise when done with strict form. If they're too difficult, you
can perform them with your knees bent, pushing from your knee instead of the foot,
or with your upper body elevated on something, such as by putting your elbow on a
bench instead of the floor.
• You can increase the resistance by putting a plate or dumbbell on your hip or
looping a band around both knees.

Seated band hip openers (external rotation-abduction)
• Lean forward to lengthen the glutes and increase tension.
• Position your feet way out with your knees together so you can combine external
rotation and abduction of the hip.
• This is not a strength exercise and progression can't be accurately tracked, so focus
on activating your glutes maximally and don't worry about the number of reps you
do. Just keep going until you can't do any more. Move up to a heavier band or
double/triple-loop the band when you go over ~30 reps per set.

64

Supine band hip internal rotation
As per the second video in this article. This is not a strength exercise and progression
can't be accurately tracked, so focus on activating your glutes maximally and don't
worry about the number of reps you do. Just keep going until you can't do any more.
Move up to a heavier band or double/triple-loop the band when you go over ~30 reps
per set.

Hip thrust from deficit
• Push from your heels to drive from your hips. This is a glute exercise, not a back
exercise. It's closer to a crunch than a squat.
• Position your feet so that your shins are roughly vertical in the top position when you
lock out your pelvis.
• Keep the tension on your glutes throughout the set: don't deload the weight on the
floor in the bottom position.
• You'll need thick bar padding for these. A regular towel or flimsy barbell coating
doesn't suffice for heavy weights. The Squat Sponge is great for this, but other thick
barbell pads can work too. You can also use an Airex Balance pad or rolled-up yoga
mat, but they're less convenient.

Unilateral hip thrust from deficit [2]
• Make sure your lower leg is roughly vertical in the top position, where you lock out
your pelvis by contracting your glutes.
• To load the exercise, place weight plates or a dumbbell on your abdomen. If you are
too strong to load the exercise this way, place a barbell in your lap like during
bilateral hip thrusts.
• Push from your heels.
• Keep the tension on your glutes throughout the set: don't deload the weight on the
floor in the bottom position.
• You'll need good bar padding for these when you get to barbell weights. A towel can
work with light weights, but for heavier weights you'll need thick barbell padding, like

65

the Squat Sponge or an Airex Balance pad. A rolled-up yoga mat can work too but is
inconvenient.

Adduction exercises
Hip adduction (not to be confused with hip abduction)
• This exercise can be performed most comfortably in a seated hip adduction
machine, but you can also use a standing hip adduction machine, cables (lying
down increases stability but compromises range of motion) or even a barbell
loaded only on one side with small plates
headings: Hip adduction (not to be confused with hip abduction)

all_headings: Prone incline lateral raise (ignore video’s instruction)
your chin on the bar. Getting your chin over the bar doesn’t suffice. You need to
Decline pull-overs with cable / dumbbell / barbell
motion. A half bosu ball is most convenient for stability.
Neck flexion with a cable / plate
Wrist pronation (best performed with an adjustable dumbbell loaded only on one side)
Wrist supination (best performed with an adjustable dumbbell loaded only on one side)
Bulgarian split squat from deficit [side angle]
lat pulldown attachment (shins on seat, ankles under the pads).
• Place your knees on a Bosu-ball or thick Airex/rubber/foam mat on the floor.
Barbell standing calf raise against the rack
• This exercise can be performed in a Smith machine, leg press or a straight-leg calf

1

PTC EXERCISE LIBRARY
Tip: Use the search function (ctrl + f) to find exercises in this document. The exercises
are also divided into rough categories.


--- Page 2 ---
2

Contents

3

3

7
Chest presses
11
Chest flys
16
Triceps isolation exercises
18
Pulls
20

25

30

32

34

37

39

40
